reasoning:
The spread recently experienced a rapid increase from around 1.05 at the end of October to 1.16 on November 7 (the latest available trading point before the November 9 cutoff). A value of 1.16 is relatively high for this series over the past month, acting as a local peak (with a max of 1.17 on Nov 4). Due to the likelihood of mean reversion after such an abrupt short-term jump, the probability that the spread will be strictly higher than this elevated threshold on future resolution dates is less than 50%. 

The FRED tool's statistical analysis provides an estimate of ~34% for an increase in the very short term (1 week). Given the volatility and mean-reverting nature of bond spreads, I am assigning a 36% probability for the 2025-11-16 resolution date. For the longer-term dates, prediction uncertainty inherently scales up, driving the probabilities closer to 0.5 (43% for 2025-12-09 and 47% for 2026-02-07), reflecting the base rate uncertainty of long-horizon market shifts but maintaining a slight lean against the currently elevated comparative baseline.
